lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market lime manufacturers that repeatedly supply external industrial, environmental, water-treatment, metals, or construction customers and provide an accountable recurring program around product specification, testing, storage, slurrying, inventory, scheduled delivery, or application support.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off or commodity-only lime sales without substantive recurring outsourced responsibilities; captive lime plants; limestone mining without lime manufacture; shells; captive internal units; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat purchase orders, bids, and delivered-price supply arrangements for quicklime, hydrated lime, and lime slurry, with eligibility limited to relationships in which specification, availability, logistics, slurrying, inventory, or application support forms a substantive ongoing supplier obligation.

a.bridge: A public lime producer's operating disclosures and broad NIST manufacturing use cases reveal workflows but do not measure wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for LMM 327410 firms. The estimate assigns exposure to planning, process monitoring, predictive maintenance, laboratory documentation, purchasing, logistics, bids, order processing, and administration while assigning little to mining, kiln operation, maintenance execution, loading, and driving; a large public producer may be more automated than LMM firms, biasing residual exposure downward.
a.rationale: Lime production requires quarrying or mining, crushing, high-temperature calcination, hydration or slurrying, storage, loading, and transport. These physical and safety-critical activities bound substitution, while NIST identifies process optimization, predictive maintenance, quality, inventory, document management, and scheduling as practical AI-enabled manufacturing workflows.
a.change_evidence: Obtain current 327410 payroll by occupation and wage, plus plant-level task studies covering control-room operators, QC labs, maintenance planners, dispatch, sales, purchasing, and administration, explicitly separating existing automation from new AI exposure.
caveats: No current six-digit occupation-by-wage table was available to directly measure the primitive.
The public-company process may be more capital intensive and automated than the injected LMM population.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis.

rho.bridge: Census measures AI use across all U.S. businesses and NIST summarizes broad manufacturing deployment, not five-year implementation of exposed lime-manufacturing work. The range discounts adoption for legacy controls, scarce process data, cyber risk, union and workforce change, MSHA and environmental compliance, continuous-operation reliability, and product-quality constraints; broad survey adoption may overstate applicability to small lime plants.
rho.rationale: Economy-wide AI use was 17%-20% in late 2025 through May 2026 and was lower among the smallest firms. Manufacturing applications are credible, but NIST lists data quality, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration as barriers, while lime plants have safety, permit, reliability, and specification obligations that require human validation.
rho.change_evidence: A plant-by-plant readiness audit of historian and sensor coverage, ERP and maintenance systems, lab-data integration, cybersecurity, operator workflows, implementation budgets, vendor pilots, and realized labor hours would materially change the range.
caveats: Neither adoption source isolates NAICS 327410 or the LMM band.
Industrial AI can initially augment workers without reducing headcount.
Unplanned downtime or quality errors at continuous kilns can outweigh labor savings, limiting deployment.

e.rationale: Lime manufacturing is fundamentally a product business, and a major producer reports orders generally made on a purchase-order or bid basis. Only a small minority of LMM firms likely provide sufficiently substantive recurring specification, inventory, slurrying, delivery, or application-management responsibilities to qualify as an outsourced service under the frozen lens.
e.change_evidence: Review customer contracts, recurring-revenue schedules, service descriptions, delivered-price terms, vendor-managed inventory, on-site silo or slurry responsibilities, and customer concentration for all ten injected LMM-band firms.
caveats: Eligibility turns on contract substance that NAICS classification does not disclose.
USGS states that more than 90% of lime consumption is chemical and industrial, but that does not make product supply a service.
The injected count of ten firms is itself a margin-bridged ESTIMATE using a Paper/Forest Products margin proxy.

s5.bridge: The public-company filing documents industry consolidation, acquisitions, and strategic transaction interest, not a denominator-based five-year transfer rate for eligible LMM firms. The range treats those facts as evidence of active strategic demand while discounting the scarcity of permitted assets, family or corporate continuity, internal transfers, and the very small eligible population; observed strategic activity may overstate liquidity for subscale plants lacking reserves or permits.
s5.rationale: The three largest companies account for more than two-thirds of North American production capacity, and United States Lime & Minerals identifies recent acquisitions and continuing evaluation of acquisitions and other transactions. This demonstrates a strategic transfer channel, but high capital, reserve, zoning, environmental, and transport barriers make each asset idiosyncratic.
s5.change_evidence: Compile five years of lime-plant and related slurry/distribution transactions from PitchBook, S&P Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, state permit transfers, and industry bankers, matched to control changes, EBITDA band, plant status, and seller ownership.
caveats: One public consolidator is not a complete market census.
Transactions may involve assets or mines rather than transferable operating firms.
With only ten injected LMM firms and a low eligibility share, realized opportunities are highly discrete.

q.rationale: Regional freight economics, permitted reserves, high plant capital, specifications, reliability, and customer proximity can support retention. A major producer raised average selling prices 5.6% in 2025, but purchase-order and bid pricing, delivered-price commitments, fuel surcharges, industrial buyer leverage, and competition should transmit a meaningful part of savings at rebid or repricing.
q.change_evidence: Examine eligible firms' contract duration, bid and renewal cadence, delivered-price versus FOB terms, fuel and energy escalators, quote win-loss history, customer concentration, gross-margin bridges, and competitor distance by plant.
caveats: Observed company pricing and margins combine volume, mix, energy, freight, acquisition, and market effects rather than AI savings.
Retention differs between scarce local supply and competitive accounts near overlapping plants.
Large industrial and public customers may negotiate prompt pass-through.

d5.bridge: USGS end-use structure and one regional public producer's sales volumes measure broad lime consumption and company-specific current demand, not five-year constant-price demand for eligible LMM service-bearing supply. The range balances diversified environmental, steel, chemical, water, and construction uses against declining coal-fired flue-gas demand and cyclical oil, steel, and construction exposure; the producer's regional growth may overstate national trend and acquisition effects may inflate volume.
d5.rationale: More than 90% of U.S. lime is now consumed in chemical and industrial uses. United States Lime & Minerals' 2025 volume rose 11.7% on construction, environmental, and steel demand, but it also reports reduced coal-related flue-gas demand, cyclicality, and weakness in oil and gas and roof shingles. Diversification supports a near-stable to modestly growing central case.
d5.change_evidence: Build an eligible-firm shipment mix by end use and region, then combine current USGS lime consumption, steel forecasts, water-treatment capital plans, environmental-compliance demand, highway budgets, data-center construction, and coal-retirement schedules through 2031 in real volume terms.
caveats: A public company's strong volume growth may reflect acquisitions, capacity additions, or share gains.
End-market diversity masks large regional and customer-specific swings.
The primitive is constant-price quantity, while company revenue also reflects pricing and mix.

o.rationale: Lime users require physical production, quality assurance, storage, delivery, and in some cases slurrying or application support. Software cannot calcine limestone, deliver material, satisfy chemical specifications, or assume environmental, mine-safety, and supply accountability. A small share could be lost to customer self-production, regenerated lime, alternative chemistries, or consolidation into larger suppliers.
o.change_evidence: Interview steel, water, environmental, chemical, and construction procurement teams about self-supply, lime regeneration, substitute reagents, vendor-managed inventory, and supplier-consolidation plans; quantify five-year substitution projects by customer.
caveats: USGS notes that some paper mills and water-treatment plants regenerate lime, but does not quantify displacement for the eligible lens.
Operator requirement is distinct from labor intensity inside the operator.
A few large customer decisions can materially affect a small regional supplier.

narrative.executive_view: Lime is a capital-intensive physical commodity with strong local logistics and permitting barriers, not naturally a recurring outsourced service. Only firms with substantive ongoing specification, inventory, slurrying, delivery, or application responsibilities qualify; AI then improves a limited information and process-control layer around an operator-heavy plant.
narrative.ai_impact: Applicable workflows include demand and production planning, kiln and energy optimization, predictive maintenance, laboratory trend analysis, safety and environmental documentation, purchasing, freight planning, bids, order processing, and customer support. Physical mining, calcination, maintenance execution, loading, and delivery remain the dominant constraints.
narrative.value_capture: Scarce permitted reserves, proximity, freight economics, product specifications, and reliability can protect savings. Purchase-order bidding, delivered-price exposure, energy and diesel volatility, strong industrial procurement, and regional competition still drive sharing at repricing.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected LMM universe contains only ten estimated firms, and most likely fail the service requirement. Strategic acquisition demand is evident in a consolidated industry, but individual plants require intensive reserve, permit, environmental, capital, labor, transport, and customer diligence.
narrative.demand_durability: Chemical, industrial, environmental, water, metals, and construction uses diversify demand. Growth in construction, steel, and environmental customers supports the current base, while coal-plant retirements, oil-and-gas cyclicality, roof-shingle weakness, and regional construction cycles create downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive uncertainty is whether any LMM firms truly bundle a recurring outsourced service rather than simply repeat product sales. Other weaknesses are the lack of six-digit occupation exposure data, sparse transfer denominators, public-company bias, high downtime consequences, environmental and MSHA obligations, energy and freight volatility, and dependence on local reserves and customers.
narrative.principal_driver: High-barrier regional supply of an indispensable physical input across diversified industrial and environmental uses.
narrative.principal_weakness: An exceptionally small LMM pool and a product-centric model leave very few firms eligible under the frozen recurring-service lens.

sources.title: Lime Statistics and Information
sources.supports: USGS describes lime's chemical, industrial, and environmental uses and states that more than 90% of current U.S. consumption is for chemical and industrial uses.

sources.title: United States Lime & Minerals, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Documents production processes, purchase-order and bid sales, 675 customers, regional transport, 2025 volume growth of 11.7% and price growth of 5.6%, competitive and permitting barriers, consolidation, acquisitions, delivered-price exposure, end-market risks, plant modernization, and employee count.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing — Text Only
sources.supports: Manufacturing AI use cases in process optimization, maintenance, quality, inventory, document management, resource planning, and scheduling, plus data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system barriers.

sources.title: Large Firms With At Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: BTOS showed overall U.S. business AI use at 17%-20% from December 2025 to May 2026, expected use at 20%-23%, and lower adoption among the smallest firms.
